public_functions.behavior_description: Initialize 3-phase complementary PWM using the high-level IfxGtm_Pwm driver. 1) Declare all configuration structs locally: main IfxGtm_Pwm_Config, an array of three IfxGtm_Pwm_ChannelConfig entries (logical channels 0..2 for phases U,V,W), an InterruptConfig object for a period-event callback, and a DTM/deadtime configuration object. Also use a persistent module-state struct (static/global) that holds: the IfxGtm_Pwm handle, a persistent channels array (size 3) that the driver will reference after init, arrays for dutyCycles[3] in percent, phases[3], and deadTimes[3] (rising/falling each) for runtime use. 2) Call IfxGtm_Pwm_initConfig to set defaults. 3) Configure each logical channelConfig[i] directly: assign the high-side output pin and its complementary low-side pin using the hardware-provided pin macros; set active states (high-active for both outputs), and enable complementary pairing. Configure deadtime to 1.0 us for both rising and falling edges and minPulse to 1.0 us via the main config’s deadtime/minPulse fields. Set alignment to center-aligned, enable syncStart and syncUpdate so all three channels start and update coherently. Assign the period-event InterruptConfig: set TOS to CPU0, priority 20, and the periodEvent callback pointer to IfxGtm_periodEventFunction; assign channelConfig[0].interrupt = &interruptConfig and channelConfig[1].interrupt = channelConfig[2].interrupt = NULL_PTR. 4) In the main config set: cluster to TOM1/Cluster_1 (TGC1), sub-module to TOM, requested frequency to 20 kHz, and select ATOM/TOM clock source as cmuFxclk0; provide the output pin descriptors for the three channels (high-side pins plus complementary pins). 5) Inside an enable guard: check IfxGtm_isEnabled. If not enabled, call IfxGtm_enable; read the GTM module frequency dynamically with IfxGtm_Cmu_getModuleFrequency; then enable the FXCLK domain clocks using IfxGtm_Cmu_enableClocks with the FXCLK mask so TOM can run. All clock steps remain inside the guard; if GTM is already enabled, skip reconfiguration. 6) Call IfxGtm_Pwm_init with the persistent channels array and the configured main config. 7) Initialize and store initial state in the module-state struct: dutyCycles = {25, 50, 75} percent; phases all zero; store the configured deadtime values and keep a reference to the channels array used by the driver. 8) Do not issue any duty update here; init applies initial duty from the configuration. 9) Configure the LED GPIO after PWM init: set the LED pin to push-pull output mode using IfxPort_setPinModeOutput; no explicit pad-driver API is available in the current template. Notes: logical channel indices 0..2 represent the three complementary pairs; each logical channel internally maps to one high-side and one complementary low-side output. Pin routing is handled in the IfxGtm_Pwm output configuration; no explicit PinMap_set* calls are used. The period callback has an empty body; the actual LED toggle on each PWM period interrupt is handled by an ISR named interruptGtmAtom, which the driver routes internally via the InterruptConfig.

Update the three phase duties in percent and apply immediately. Algorithm per call: for each dutyCycles[i] (i = 0..2), compute next = dutyCycles[i] + stepPercent (10). If (dutyCycles[i] + stepPercent) >= 100, first set dutyCycles[i] = 0, then add stepPercent (resulting in stepPercent). This enforces the wrap rule: if the sum reaches 100 or more, wrap to 0 then add the step. Store the updated duties back to the module-state array. Pass the state’s dutyCycles array directly (percent values) to the high-level API IfxGtm_Pwm_updateChannelsDutyImmediate to request an immediate update. The driver will schedule the change coherently for the next PWM period in center-aligned mode. No STM timing or delays are handled here; scheduling at 500 ms cadence is performed in Cpu0_Main.
Period-event callback invoked by the high-level PWM driver on each PWM period event for the base (first) logical channel. Requirements specify an empty body; no processing is performed here.

ISR function routed internally by the high-level PWM driver through the ATOM/TOM interrupt path associated with the PWM period event. It performs a single action: toggle the LED output pin each time it runs using IfxPort_togglePin. Priority is configured via the PWM driver’s InterruptConfig (priority 20, TOS CPU0). Do not call any driver-level interrupt handler here.

header_selection.reconciliation_reason: LLM driver_calls use IfxGtm_Pwm_* API, not IfxGtm_Tom_PwmHl (the reference API). Updated to match actual usage.
